coredata.dc:title: Starch fermentation wastewater as a precursor to prepare S,N-doped carbon dots for selective Fe(III) detection and carbon microspheres for solution decolorization
coredata.dc:creator: Man, Yun
Li, Zuomei
Kong, Wei-Li
Li, Weihua
Dong, Weiwei
Wang, Yaqin
Xie, Fazhi
Zhao, Donglin
Qu, Qishu
Zou, Wen-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Resource utilization of starch fermentation wastewater is facing the challenge that is short of trash to treasure approach with high efficiency except for the fodder production. Here we reported the use of starch fermentation wastewater as a precursor to prepare carbon materials. When the carbonization temperature being set at 180 °C, the S,-N-doped C-dots with ~24.5% of quantum yield and a green emission at ~518 nm were obtained. The functional groups on the surface of S,-N-doped C-dots can coordinate with free ferric ion (Fe(III)), and the excited electron of C-dots can be captured by 3d orbit of Fe(III), which, together with the assistance of inner filter effect, resulted in the quenching of green fluorescence of S,-N-doped C-dots. A sensing platform for Fe(III) was therefore established with a 3.2 nM detection limit in 0.01 to 16 µM concentration range. When the carbonization temperature being enhanced to 200 °C, the carbon microspheres with ~3.3–6.9 μm in diameter were gained. The carbon microspheres own benign outer hydrophilicity and inner hydrophobicity, and can efficiently remove the dyes in solution through π-π stacking, hydrogen bond and electrostatic effect, exhibiting a good adsorption capacity with ~188 mg/g and a rapid absorption equilibrium within 60 min. The S,N-doped C-dots and carbon microspheres were applied in Fe(III) determination in environmental samples and solution decolorization, respectively, demonstrating their good applicability, and opening up a promising door for the magnificent transition from fermentation wastewater to valuable resource.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Starch fermentation wastewater
S, N-doped carbon dots
Carbon microspheres
Fe(III) sensing
Solution decolorization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of C-dots and carbon microspheres
Analysis for Fe(III) of real-world water samples
Preparation and characterization of C-dots using DDS as a precursor
Fluorescent responsiveness and selectivity to Fe(III) of S,N-doped C-dots
Figures merit of C-dots in real sample assay
Preparation and characterization of carbon microspheres using DDS as raw materials
Possible mechanisms of response to Fe(III) of S,N-doped C-dots and solution decolorization of carbon microspheres

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Doctoral scientific research foundation of Anhui Jianzhu University (2017QD15, 2018QD60), and Foundation of Anhui Provincial Education Department (KJ2019A0775) for financial support. The authors also gratefully acknowledge Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province ( 1908085MB40 ), and National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51978003 , 21777001 , 21876001 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FTIR (A) and XPS (B) spectra of S,N-doped C-dots. High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (C), N1s (D), O1s (E) and S2p (F) of S,N-doped C-dots.
Power X-ray diffraction pattern (A) and transmission electron microscopy image (B) of the S,N-doped C-dots.

Spectral properties of S,N-doped C-dots. Normalized UV–vis absorption, excitation and emission spectra of S,N-doped C-dots (A). Inset is the photograph taken under 365 nm UV lamp. Fluorescence decay of S,N-doped C-dots in aqueous solution (B). The pH-dependent emission of C-dots. Inset is the plots of intensity vs pH (C). Normalized excitation-dependent emission of S,N-doped C-dots (D).

Fluorescence spectra of Fe(III) titrating S,N-doped C-dots aqueous solution (A) Insets are the corresponding plots of lg(F0/F) vs Fe(III) concentration within the range of 0.01–16.0 μM (Top) and the photographs of the S,N-doped C-dots aqueous solution before and after 20 μM Fe(III) under 365 nm UV light (Bottom). The selectivity of S,N-doped C-dots chemosensor for Fe(III) over other ones in pH ~7.0 (B). Metal ions from 1 to 13 represent NH4 +, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Pd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, respectively.
SEM (A) and EDX (B) images of carbon microsphere. The inset in B is the element composition of carbon microsphere.

Power X-ray diffraction pattern (A), FTIR spectrum (B) and high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (C) of the carbon microsphere. The UV–vis spectra of MB before and after the addition of the carbon microsphere (D). The insets in D are the photographs of MB aqueous solution and adding carbon microsphere for 60 min, respectively.

Normalized Uv–vis spectra of S,N-doped C-dots aqueous solution and mixed solution of S,N-doped C-dots and Fe(III) (A). The concentrations of S,N-doped C-dots and Fe(III) are 27 mg/mL and 0.1 mM, respectively. Schematic diagram of electron-accepting mechanism of 3d orbit of Fe(III) (B).
Schematic presentation of the preparation of S,N-doped C-dots using starch fermentation wastewater as a precursor.

head.ce:title: Starch fermentation wastewater as a precursor to prepare S,N-doped carbon dots for selective Fe(III) detection and carbon microspheres for solution decolorization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Bengbu University, 1866, Caoshan Road, Bengbu, Anhui 233030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Food and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Food and Biological Engineering, Bengbu University, 1866, Caoshan Road, Bengbu, Anhui 233030, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interface Process, and Anhui Key Laboratory of water pollution control and wastewater reuse, Anhui Jianzhu University, 292 Ziyun Road, Hefei, Anhui 230022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interface Process, and Anhui Key Laboratory of water pollution control and wastewater reuse

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Functional Molecule Design and Interface Process, and Anhui Key Laboratory of water pollution control and wastewater reuse, Anhui Jianzhu University, 292 Ziyun Road, Hefei, Anhui 230022, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Yun Man and Zuomei Li equally contributed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resource utilization of starch fermentation wastewater is facing the challenge that is short of trash to treasure approach with high efficiency except for the fodder production. Here we reported the use of starch fermentation wastewater as a precursor to prepare carbon materials. When the carbonization temperature being set at 180 °C, the S,-N-doped C-dots with ~24.5% of quantum yield and a green emission at ~518 nm were obtained. The functional groups on the surface of S,-N-doped C-dots can coordinate with free ferric ion (Fe(III)), and the excited electron of C-dots can be captured by 3d orbit of Fe(III), which, together with the assistance of inner filter effect, resulted in the quenching of green fluorescence of S,-N-doped C-dots. A sensing platform for Fe(III) was therefore established with a 3.2 nM detection limit in 0.01 to 16 µM concentration range. When the carbonization temperature being enhanced to 200 °C, the carbon microspheres with ~3.3–6.9 μm in diameter were gained. The carbon microspheres own benign outer hydrophilicity and inner hydrophobicity, and can efficiently remove the dyes in solution through π-π stacking, hydrogen bond and electrostatic effect, exhibiting a good adsorption capacity with ~188 mg/g and a rapid absorption equilibrium within 60 min. The S,N-doped C-dots and carbon microspheres were applied in Fe(III) determination in environmental samples and solution decolorization, respectively, demonstrating their good applicability, and opening up a promising door for the magnificent transition from fermentation wastewater to valuable resource.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based luminescent nanomaterials, that is carbon dots (C-dots), have received an increasing amount of scientific attention due to their significant properties as follows: 1) Favourable physicochemical properties: such as high chemical inertness, nice aqueous solubility, prominent photostability, well biocompatibility, and so on [1–3]. In comparison to common semiconducting quantum dots with high cytotoxicity, C-dots have the potentials to replace their applications in the fields of biosensing, bioimaging, drug and gene delivery, etc., which were considered as the next generation contrast agent [4–8]; 2) Simple synthesis condition and purification process: natural biomass or chemical reagents were utilized to fabricate highly bright C-dots via a straightforward dialysis after simple microwavepyrolysis, hydrothermal carbonization, extraction, thermal decomposition and chemical oxidation, etc. The same troublesome separation and purification operations as the fabrication of organic dyes were directly omitted [9–11]; 3) Cheap raw materials extremely easy to obtain at one’s fingertips: different from the need of highly purified raw materials and the use of minority of rare elements of metal-based quantum dots and upconversion nanoparticles, the precursors used to the preparation of C-dots, including insecticide [12,13], hair fiber [14], carbon black[15,16], milk [17], glycine [18], carbohydrates [19,20], resol [21], glycerol [22], candle soot [23], fruits [24–26], and so on, have been reported, and used as received without further purification; 4) Tunable surface chemistry and multicolor emission: the C-Dots doped with sulphur, nitrogen, phosphorus, boron, etc., or various combinations significantly influence their excited-state dynamics, resulting in an extended π-conjugation and a longer wavelength fluorescence [27,28]. The emission of C-dots is greatly affected by the electronic band gap transitions of conjugated-domains, surface defect states and element doping [29,30]; 5) Wide application prospect: at present, C-dots have been applied in theranostics [31], chemical and biological sensing [32–37], energy conversion and storage [38], antimicrobial [39], catalysis [40], and so on. In the face of the situation like wildfirearound the world, it is still highly demanded for the innovative synthesis and revolutionary application of C-dots.

Direct fermentation of aged grain, corn, cassava, etc., for fuel alcohol as a strategic reserve technology has been paid more attentionby many countries all thewordthan before, which not only reduced the wasting of resources, but also efficiently remitted theenergyshortage situation [41]. Starch fermentation process produced a good deal of wastewater. The distillersdriedgrains (DDG) and distillersdried solubles(DDS) were obtained via pressure-filter treatment [42]. The former mainly composes of insoluble substance, such as cereal debris and thallus containing protein, fiber and lipid. The latter is the seriflux concentrated to 35% of liquid portion, and the dominant ingredients are small peptide, amino acids, vitamin, etc., nutrient content, which has high chemical oxygen demand (COD), biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), and doesn’t directly discharge. Current trash to treasure approach is spraying the DDS concentrated to 35% into the drying DDG to form distillersdriedgrains with solubles (DDGS) fodder. However, the concentration cost of DDS is so high that the price of making waste profitable is very giant. Taking into account the cost of drying the mixture of DDG and DDS, the price will be much higher. Moreover, due to the sugar-rich DDS, great appending proportion of DDS into DDG makes the fodder deep color, thus influences the quality of products. The DDS-saving will occupy huge inventory after long-time accumulation, affecting further production. Therefore, it is urgent and necessary to seek much more efficient or complementary pathway to treat starch fermentation wastewater.

Herein S,N-doped C-dots and carbon microspheres were synthesized by a one-pot hydrothermal method using concentrated DDS as a precursor at different temperature for 10 h. The as-prepared S,N-doped C-dots carbonized at 180 °C owned a ~24.5% of QY at ~520 nm wavelength. Also, the newly synthesized C-dots displayed pH- and excitation-dependent emission. The functional groups on the surface of C-dots, such as –OH and –COOH, etc., can efficiently coordinate with Fe(III), the captured Fe(III) can accept the excited electron of S,N-doped C-dots, resulting in the quenching of the fluorescence. Thus, Fe(III) can be selectively determined in the concentration range of 0.01–16 μM with a detection limit of 3.2 nM. When the carbonization temperature was enhanced to 200 °C, the carbon microspheres were obtained, which displayed an excellent solution decolorization performance using methylene blue as a model dye.

The precursor was the seriflux concentrated to 35%, which is the soluble substance in starch fermentation waste via pressure-filter treatment. Metal ions salts and sodium phosphate (purity 99%) are acquired from J&K Chemical LTD. (Shanghai, China) and Alfa Aesar (Tianjin, China), respectively. All other reagents were of analytical and used without further purification.

Fluorescence spectra were measured on an F-4600 spectrofluorophotometer equipped with a Xe lamp (Hitachi, Tokyo, Japan). The fluorescence lifetime were recorded on a HORIBA FluoroHub Analytical Instrument. UV–vis absorption spectra were measured by a Shimadzu 3100 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). FTIR spectra were measured using a Nicolet-6700 spectrograph (Nicolet, Madison, WI, USA). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was taken on a JEOL JEM- 200CX (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) transmission electron microscope. The powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was performed on Shimadzu XRD-6000 diffractometer by using Cu Kα (λ = 0.154056 Å) as the incident radiation. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded on a ThermoESCALAB250 multifunctional photoelectron spectrometer. The zeta-potential was analyzed by a Zetasizer Nano ZS instrument (Malvern, U.K.). The pH measurement of solution was conducted by a Sartorius PB-10 pH meter (Sartorius, Dietikon, Switzerland).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots and carbon microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: Briefly, 0.03 g of concentrated DDS was dispersed in 20 mL double distilled water (DDW). The solution was centrifuged for 10 min to remove insoluble substance followed by transfer to the Teflon-lined autoclave, which was then heated for 10 h at different temperature. The naturally cooled solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the large particles. The supernatant was subsequently concentrated at 60 °C prior to freeze-dry to form powder or re-disperse in DDW to form 100 mg/mL solution. The C-dots were preserved in refrigerator at 4 °C for further use. Especially mentioned, the precursor solution for carbon microspheres was modulated as 50 mg/mL. The carbon microspheres obtained at 200 °C were directly centrifuged and dried in oven at 80 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis for Fe(III) of real-world water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real-world water samples from Yuwan wastewater treatment plant, Yihai lake in Anhui Jianzhu University, tap in lab were filtered through 0.45 μm Supor filters. The mother liquors adjusted pH to ~7.0 were collected in a precleaned glass bottle, which was stored in a refrigerator to further use.

The experiment was carried out in glass beaker at room temperature and pH ~7.0. 20 mL of methylene blue (MB) solution with 10 mg/L was shaked with carbon microspheres with 50 mg/L on a shaker at 250 rpm. The equilibrium concentration of dyes was measured with UV–vis spectrophotometer at maximum absorbance. Using the Lambert-Beer law as listed below and its absorption maximum to a predetermined standard curve of MB, the final concentration of the dyes in solution can be calculated: A = εbc.

According to the 13th Five-Year plan of China, yield of fuel ethanol by starch fermentation will reach 400 million tons in 2020. Data provided by State Statistics Bureau of China, in 2017, total output of enterprise above designated size of nationwide liquor-making industry was 7077.41 ten thousand kiloliter, year-on-year growth was 3.31%. Of which the yield of beverage alcohol was 6050.13 ten thousand kiloliter, year-on-year growth was 0.97%, and as for that of fermentation alcohol, 1027.29 ten thousand kiloliter and 19.63%, respectively. It is conceivable the treatment and utilization toward DDGS will be troublesome in the near future. Besides deepening the present technology of DDGS for fodder, the exploration of other processing approach with high efficiency and high added-value has become the choice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of C-dots using DDS as a precursor
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the carbonization temperature is relatively low, it is hard to convert precursors to C-dots. On the other hand, when the temperature is much high, resulting in powders in the microscale or bulk materials with high carbonization degree, but appropriate one, C-dots in the nanoscale [43–46]. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , different temperatures from 160 to 240 °C with an interval of 20 °C, were attempted to the preparation of carbon nanomaterials using starch fermentation wastewater as a precursor. The obtained cyan C-dots looked like very weak, even no fluorescence at 160 °C (see Scheme 1A). This approach was directly neglected due to the lack of value-in-use and much lower QY. Reversely, the obtained C-dots took on brightly green fluorescence at 180 °C. The Zeta-Potential, FTIR, XPS, TEM, XRD, etc., were therefore employed to characterize the surface charge, composition, morphology and crystalline state of the C-dots. The Zeta-Potential of C-dots was measured as −6.91 mV (see Fig. S1), indicating the negatively charged surface of C-dots, also signifying –COO−, –SOx −, etc., anchored on the surface based on the raw material containing sugar and amino acids. The characteristic absorption peaks of O–H and C–H at 3406 and 2952 cm−1, respectively, were observed on the FTIR curve (see Fig. 1 A), indicating the presence of many hydroxyl groups possibly derived from sugar on the C-dots surface and carbon skeleton, respectively [12,35]. The absorption peak at 1042 cm−1 is attributed to alkoxy C-OH stretching vibration, and -SO3 −, C-O-C bonds [19]. The absorption between 1440 and 1310 cm−1 can be identified as COO−, aromatic CC and C-N groups [47]. In addition, the peak at 1643 and 1135 cm−1 may be attributed to the CO stretching vibration, and C–N and C–S bonds, respectively [48].

The XPS spectra were conducted to further testify the same surface functional groups as FTIR one and demonstrated the possible existence of other groups. Fig. 1B reveals the C-dots were composed of carbon (~285 eV), nitrogen (~400.9 eV), oxygen (~531.5 eV) and sulfur (~163.5 eV) four elements, forming the present S,N-doped C-dots. The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of S,N-doped dots shown in Fig. 1C diplays five peaks with the binding energy of about ~284.6, ~285.2, ~285.8, ~286.2 and ~288.2 eV, are assigned to C–C/CC, C-S, C-N/COOR, C-OH and CO species, respectively [49]. The three peaks at ~400.0, ~400.9 and ~401.7 eV on the N1s spectrum are identified as the amide N, pyrrolic N and graphitic N, respectively [50] (see Fig. 1D). The O1s spectrum shown in Fig. 2 E mainly consists of three peaks at ~531.3, ~531.8 and ~532.8 eV, which are attributed to –C–O–, –SOx and –CO, respectively. As shown in Fig. 1F, the two peaks centered at ~163 eV and ~168 eV on the high-resolution XPS spectrum curve of S2p was observed, manifesting that sulfur exists in two types of covalence. The peak with low binding energy decomposed into two distinct components at ~163.1 and ~164.3 eV through spin–orbit couplings, that is the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 of thiophene S [51]. The other peak can be fitted into three components at ~167.6, ~168.5, and ~169.2 eV, corresponding to the –C–SOx– (x = 2, 3, 4) groups, respectively [52,53]. The sulfur element was speculated to possibly root in sugar containing –SO3 − and/or amino acid containing –HS in DDS precursor. These findings indicated the functional groups as following, –COOH, -SOx (x = 2, 3, 4), –NH2/–NH-, -C-OH, etc., integrated on the surface of the C-dots (see Scheme 1B). The XRD pattern of S,N-doped C-dots displays a diffraction peak at 25.5° (see Fig. 2A), indicating an interlayer spacing of 0.36 nm, which possibly ascribed to the generation of C-O-C, COOR, -SOx etc.[36]. As shown in Fig. 2B, a monodispersion state and nearly spherical morphology were observed on the TEM image. The statistical particle size of C-dots was mainly distributed in the range of 2.3–6.7 nm with an average diameter of 4.2 nm.

As a next-generation contrast agent, the photoluminescent properties of C-dots determine the potential in cellular imaging. The normalized UV–vis curve shown in Fig. 3 A reveals the C-dots have obvious adsorption from ultraviolet to visible regions. The adsorption less than ~260 nm probably arises from the π → π* transition of the carbon cores. The absorption band centered at ~278 nm can be attributed to the n → π* transition of oxygen atom of carbonyl. The shoulder absorption from 310 to 375 nm is responsible for the trapping of excited state energy by the surface states [12,35,54]. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of C-dots centered at ~460 and ~518 nm, respectively, were observed. Accordingly, under the irradiation of a 365 nm UV lamp, the C-dots aqueous solution took on a brightly green fluorescence (see inset in Fig. 3A). The lifetime decay curve was also measured to gain insight into the optical property of aqueous S,N-doped C-dots. As shown in Fig. 3B, the decay curve fits well by a tri-exponential function, which indicated the presence of three decay channels responsible for the observed photoluminescence pattern. From the decay components, the mean lifetime was calculated as 3.84 ns. The QY of the C-dots highly correlates with raw materials, synthesis method and the surface chemistry involved [55]. The QY of S,N-doped C-dots was fitted as ~0.245 by the slope method using quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, QY = 0.54) as a reference (see Fig. S2). The visible excitation and emission, relatively high QY, wastewater as raw materials signified the S,N-doped C-dots have a great potential for application in analyte sensing.

On the one hand, during carbonization process, the residual amino acids in starch fermentation wastewater partially decorated at the surface of the C-dots, and water took part in the redox reaction to form –COOH, –CO [56], etc. resulting in the varying isoelectric points and dissociation constants of C-dots similar to ampholytes [13,35,36]. On the other hand, due to the quantum confinement effect of C-dots, emissive traps and the electronic conjugate structure of C-dots can influence the emission [57]. These two led to the pH- and excitation-dependent emission behaviors of C-dots, respectively. It can be seen from Fig. 3C, the emission of C-dots firstly enhanced with pH from 2.0 to 4.0, and gradually decreased with pH from 7.0 to 13.0 via an optimal and stable pH range from 4.0 to 7.0 (see inset in Fig. 3C). Similarly, the emission peak of S,N-doped C-dots synchronously changed with excitation, that is with the excitation wavelength being increased from 370 to 510 nm, the emission correspondingly red-shifted from 505 to 595 nm (see Fig. 3D and Fig. S3). The maximum excitation/emission wavelengths centered at 460/518 nm were observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent responsiveness and selectivity to Fe(III) of S,N-doped C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The surface of S,N-doped C-dots decorated by activity groups held the potential to coordinate with metal ions. Initially, it was considered the feasibility of using this C-dots for life ion Fe(III) detection, mainly because Fe(III) is an important nutrition element of most organisms and the most frequently used material in daily life. The effect of Fe(III) on the fluorescence intensity of S,N-doped C-dots was therefore explored. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with Fe(III) being injected into S,N-doped C-dots aqueous solution, the intensity of fluorescence at 518 nm was gradually quenched, and the fluorescence was quenched by ~72% when Fe(III) concentration was 20 μM, (see bottom Inset of Fig. 4A). The S,N-doped C-dots were so sensitive response to Fe(III) that it can be a promising platform for Fe(III) quantitative assay. The effect of pH on Fe(III) detection was considered. When the Fe(III) concentration was mixed at 10.0 µM, and pH was changed from 4.0 to 8.0 with an interval of 1.0, the biggest quenching efficiency (F0/F) occurred in pH ~5.0, others are lower 6.0% or so than that of optimal pH (data not shown). Moreover, 27.0 mg/mL of S,N-doped C-dots displayed a high enough brightness (~1000 a.u.). Overall consideration, 27.0 mg/mL of S,N-doped C-dots concentration and pH 7.0 buffered with Tris-HCl were selected for all experimental conveniences. The plots of lg(F0/F) vs Fe(III) concentration displayed a good linear relationship in the range of 0.01–16.0 μM (see top Inset of Fig. 4A), and the linear fitting equation is Y = 0.02715C + 0.00148, R = 0.9986. Where Y is the logarithm value of quenching efficiency F0/F, and C is the Fe(III) concentration in μM. F0 and F represent the original and instant fluorescence intensity at 518 nm, respectively. The limit of detection (S/N = 3) was estimated to be 3.2 nM. 3.8% of relative standard derivation (RSD) was figured out by 10.0 μM of Fe(III) detections for five times, demonstrating the reliability of S,N-doped C-dots.

The responsiveness and selectivity as two sides of the same coin are indispensible toward a new sensing platform. It must be considerable the possible interference with the determination of Fe(III) of coexisting common metal ions in the real samples. As shown in Fig. 4B, compared with ~72% of quenching ratio caused by 14.0 μM of Fe(III), the metal ions with relatively high concentration, such as 1.0 mM of NH4 +, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, and 20.0 μM of Zn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, etc., led to lower than 20% of quenched ratio only, indicating that the S,N-doped C-dots hold much higher selectivity for Fe(III) over other metal ions. Also, the competition experiments verified the excellent selectivity to Fe(III) (see Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Figures merit of C-dots in real sample assay
ce:section.ce:para: On the basis of the observation that the fluorescence of S,N-doped C-dots was efficiently quenched by Fe(III), the developed chemosensor was additionally applied for detecting Fe(III) in real water samples. Three different concentrations 4.0, 8.0 and 12.0 μM of Fe(III) were piked into real samples from Yuwan waste water treatment plant, Yihai lake in Anhui Jianzhu University and tip water, respectively, for Fe(III) assay with the current method. Prior to measurement, the residual Fe(III) amount in water samples must be learned by the injection of same concentration of S,N-doped C-Dots into water samples and DDW. The nearly changeless fluorescence intensities revealed no Fe(III) or lower than limit of detection in real-world water samples. The measured results are in line with the spiked Fe(III) concentrations, and the quantitative recovery ratios from 96.2% to 104.5% were found, further testifying the reliability and feasibility of S,N-doped C-dots for Fe(III) assay in real water samples (see Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of carbon microspheres using DDS as raw materials
ce:section.ce:para: The bulk carbon was obtained when carbonization temperature were set over 200 °C (Data not shown). The carbon materials carbonized at 200 °C were emphatically characterized due to the smaller size and greater surface-volume ratio. SEM image shown in Fig. 5 A reveals that the obtained carbon materials are spherical, widely distributed, and well dispersed, with size about 3.3–6.9 μm in diameter. Also, the EDX was employed to analyze the chemical composition of the carbon microspheres. The EDX spectrum reveals the carbon microspheres were composed of carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and sulfur (see Fig. 5B), and the atomic percent was 55.65, 23.05, 19.28 and 0.03, respectively. The XRD pattern is the characteristic of graphite with a (002) layer spacing identical to that of bulk graphite (3.34 Å) and (100) one (see Fig. 6 A) [58]. Obviously, the enhanced carbonization temperature led to the crystal form from amorphous carbon at 180 °C to graphitization one at 200 °C. The FTIR spectrum uncovers the general surface functional groups of carbon microspheres (see Fig. 6B). The strong characteristic absorption band of O–H at 3416 indicates the presence of many hydroxyl groups on the surface of carbon microspheres, signifying its excellent hydrophilic performance [12,35]. Compared with that of S,N-doped C-dots, both display the peak at ~1038 cm−1 assigned to alkoxy C-OH stretching vibration, and -SO3 −, C-O-C bonds [19], and the peak at ~1132 cm−1 attributed to the C-N and C-N groups [47]. The maximal difference lay in the occurrence of quadruple peak within 1450–1650 cm−1, which is the characteristic vibration of the benzene ring skeleton, coinciding with the graphitization structure of carbon microspheres. In addition, the XPS spectrum of carbon microspheres takes on the same profile as that of S,N-doped C-dots (see Fig. S5). The high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum of carbon microspheres shown in Fig. 6C was analyzed, which displays five peaks with the binding energy of about ~284.6, ~285.2, ~285.8, ~286.2 and ~288.2 eV, are assigned to C–C/CC, C–S, C–N/COOR, C–OH and CO species, respectively [50]. The much stronger aromatic C–C/CC peak also testifies the graphitization structure of carbon microspheres. The zeta-potential result demonstrates the carbon microspheres negatively charged (~−50 mV) (see Fig. S6).

As known, the key factor of solution decolorization depends on their own performances of absorbent and dye. Also, the concentrations of both above, interaction time and pH value, etc., influence on the adsorptive property [59]. As a basic organic dye of the phenothiazine class, Methylene blue (MB) is highly water soluble and widely used as a colorant in textiles and food stuffs. It was reported that the MB-containing wastewater does severe harm to the environment and human health. Therefore, it is of great importance to remove the MB in waste water. Except under special circumstances, the pH of natural water bodies is close to ~7.0. In consideration of the factors above and the limitation of length of article, MB was employed to investigate the absorption capacity of carbon microspheres, and the concentrations of absorbent and dye, and pH value of solution was set as 50 and 10 mg/L, and ~7.0, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6D, the initial MB displayed a brightly blue color. After the addition of carbon microspheres into MB aqueous solution and shake for 10 min, the disappearance of solution color was observed, meaning the MB were almost totally absorbed by the carbon microspheres. The MB aqueous solution was entirely decolorized in 60 min (see Inset in Fig. 6D). The maximum adsorption capacity at 298 K was calculated as 188 mg/g. The results above demonstrate that the carbon microspheres can quickly and efficiently adsorbed MB dye in aqueous solution, providing a new approach for the removal of dye in waste water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible mechanisms of response to Fe(III) of S,N-doped C-dots and solution decolorization of carbon microspheres
ce:section.ce:para: The exploration on the possible mechanism response to Fe(III) of S,N-doped C-dots will help to promote their application. As shown in Fig. 7 A, when 0.1 mM of Fe(III) was injected into 27 mg/mL of S,N-doped C-dots aqueous solution, the profile of UV–vis spectrum obtained is the same as that of single C-dots aqueous solution. The difference lay in the absorption enhancement at wavelength lower than 500 nm. The slight overlap from 325 to 500 nm of Uv–vis and excitation spectra was observed, testifying the inner filter effect was not the dominant contribution to the fluorescence quenching. Fe(III) has strong electron-accepting ability and can capture electrons more easily. It was reported the fluorescence of fluorophore can be quenched by an electron transfer in the excited state and coordination of Fe(III) [60,61]. The above weak UV–vis spectrum of Fe(III) titrating S,N-doped C-dots aqueous solution indicated the existence of coordination interaction between two. Moreover, the addition of phosphate into the S,N-doped C-dots aqueous solution buffered with HCl-tris at pH 7.0 and Fe(III) mixed system caused the revival of quenched fluorescence (see Fig. S7), also demonstrating such interaction. The contrast experiments were gave the same result, which helps to better learn the quenching mechanism (see Fig. S8). Therefore, as well as inner filter effect, it was believed Fe(III) added into the S,N-doped C-dots aqueous solution can coordinate with functional groups on the surface of C-dots (Scheme 1B), and the electrons in excited state of S,N-doped C-dots will transfer to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe(III), resulting in the significant fluorescence quenching (see Fig. 7B) [61,62].

The higher adsorption capacity of carbon microspheres for MB may be attributed to three factors as follows: 1) the functional groups such as –COO− and –SOX − on the surface of carbon microspheres are negatively charged, which can capture MB cationic dye through electrostatic interaction; 2) a large amount of hydroxyl groups anchored on the surface of carbon microspheres, which used as hydrogen bond donor to interaction with hydrogen bond acceptor such as nitrogen atom, π electron of MB, resulting in the MB molecules were adsorbed on the surface of carbon microspheres; 3) MB has rich aromatic rings, while the carbon microspheres featured much hydrophobic sites [6], which can adsorb dye molecules on the large specific surface area through π-π stacking.

In summary, DDS was used as a precursor to prepare carbon materials at different temperature and for fixed time. The C-dots with weakly blue emission, ones with brightly green emission, carbon microspheres and bulk carbon were obtained when temperature was set as 160, 180, 200 and over 200 °C, respectively. The S,-N-doped C-dots carbonized at 180 °C can selectively response to Fe(III) mainly through accepting the excited electron of S,-N-doped C-dots by its 3d orbit of Fe(III), resulting in the selective quenching of fluorescence of S,-N-doped C-dots, and then the establishment of the sensing platform for Fe(III). The negatively charged carbon microspheres formed a large amount of hydrophobic sites and was decorated by –OH groups during carbonization process, promoting the efficient removal of dye molecules in solution through π-π stacking, hydrogen bond and electrostatic interaction. The obtained carbon materials were successfully applied in the Fe(III) detection in environmental samples and solution decolorization, demonstrating the reliability of the new way to turn waste into treasure.

Yun Man: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Zuomei Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation. Wei-Li Kong: Project administration. Weihua Li: Validation, Funding acquisition. Weiwei Dong: Investigation. Yaqin Wang: Methodology, Investigation. Fazhi Xie: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Donglin Zhao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Qishu Qu: Methodology, Investigation. Wen-Sheng Zou: Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Doctoral scientific research foundation of Anhui Jianzhu University(2017QD15, 2018QD60), and Foundation of Anhui Provincial Education Department (KJ2019A0775) for financial support. The authors also gratefully acknowledge Natural Science Foundation of Anhui Province (1908085MB40), and National Natural Science Foundation of China (51978003, 21777001, 21876001).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105338.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.L. Bricks, A. Kovalchuk, C. Trieflinger, M. Nofz, M. Buschel, A.I. Tolmachev, J. Daub, K. Rurack, On the development of sensor molecules that display Fe(III)-amplified fluorescence, Journal of the American Chemical Society, 127 (2005) 13522-13529.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent graphene oxide logic gates for discrimination of iron (3+) and iron (2+) in living cells by imaging
Chem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Mei, C. Jiang, G. Guan, K. Zhang, B. Liu, R. Liu, Z. Zhang, Fluorescent graphene oxide logic gates for discrimination of iron (3+) and iron (2+) in living cells by imaging, Chemical Communications, 48 (2012) 7468-7470.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+
Anal. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Li, Y. Li, J. Cao, J. Zhu, L. Fan, X. Li, Sulfur-Doped Graphene Quantum Dots as a Novel Fluorescent Probe for Highly Selective and Sensitive Detection of Fe3+, Analytical Chemistry, 86 (2014) 10201-10207.
